1:
The chapter opens with the quiet sadness of a great man growing old, and the witnesses linger over both the fact and its lesson. Gill gathers the Jewish reckonings of Samuel's age: the common notion that he lived but fifty-two years — in which case the infirmities of age came early, 'through his indefatigable labours from his childhood, and the cares and burdens of government' — while others say about sixty, and Abarbinel more than seventy; the rabbinic rule being that a man is called old at sixty and grey-headed at seventy. Ellicott, laying out the five periods of Samuel's life from the child in the Tabernacle to the judge of a free nation, inclines with 'a famous Hebrew commentator' toward seventy. Matthew Henry and Wesley care less for the arithmetic than for the moral: Samuel 'was a man betimes,' full of thoughts and cares when he was a child, and had spent his strength in the fatigue of public business, so that 'if he think to shake himself as at other times, he finds he is mistaken: old age has cut his hair.' Both draw the same application — those in the prime of their years ought to be busy in the work of life, for as they go into years they will be less disposed to it and less able for it.

On the making of his sons judges the witnesses agree in limiting the office and defending the father. Clarke insists they were his lieutenants at Beer-sheba, 'never judges in the proper sense of the word; Samuel was the last judge in Israel, and he judged it to the day of his death' (1 Samuel 7:15). Wesley concurs: not supreme judges, 'for such there was to be but one, and that of God's chusing,' but deputies, with a right of appeal reserved to Samuel himself. Henry clears Samuel of nepotism — he had no more ambition to entail the government upon his family than Gideon had — and supposes the southern towns petitioned for resident judges to spare them long journeys with their causes; the sons, for aught that yet appeared, were fit men, respected for their good father's sake.

Keil and Delitzsch set the verse in the larger sweep of the history: the earthly monarchy in Israel was established 'in the time of Samuel, and through his mediation,' and this chapter introduces the whole account of Saul's rise. Ellicott adds the political shadow — as old age enfeebled Samuel and duties fell to worthless sons, the Philistines were probably recovering their power over the southern districts. Henry's summary carries the pastoral weight the others share: here are 'two sad things, but not strange things' — a good and useful man growing old and unfit for service, and the children of a good man turning aside and not treading in his steps. The stage is set for Israel's demand, and the witnesses want the reader to feel how humanly understandable, and yet how spiritually dangerous, the moment was.
2:
The bare register of names draws out more from the witnesses than one might expect. Ellicott hears in the names themselves 'the holy atmosphere their father lived in': Joel signifies Jehovah is God, and Abiah Jehovah a Father — a devout man's hopes stamped on his children, which makes the sequel the more bitter, for 'the glorious traditions of Samuel were quickly forgotten by these unworthy men who called him father.' Keil and Delitzsch confirm the names as genuine Hebrew formations and pass at once to the fact that the sons were judges in Beersheba, where their fall is recorded in the very next breath of the narrative.

Gill attends to a small textual knot: in 1 Chronicles 6:28 the firstborn appears under the name Vashni, which he harmonises with the Joel of this verse; and he warns against a tempting identification — this Joel is not Joel the prophet, 'neither his parentage, nor his office, nor his times, will agree with this.' The geography interests Wesley: Beer-sheba lay 'in the southern border of the land of Canaan,' very remote from Samuel's house at Ramah, while Samuel himself still executed the office of judge in the north and its neighbouring places. Ellicott supplements from Josephus that only one son remained at Beer-sheba while the other judged in the north of the land, so that the aged prophet's circuit was effectively parcelled out between them.

That very distance becomes, for Matthew Henry, the key to the tragedy that follows. The southern towns, he supposes, had asked for judges of their own so they need not travel far with their causes; but 'when Samuel's sons were made judges, and settled at a distance from him, then they discovered themselves.' Many who conduct themselves well under their parents' eye prove bad when they go abroad into the world and set up for themselves; and many who did well in a state of meanness have been spoiled by preferment and power, for 'honours change men's minds, and too often for the worse.' So the witnesses read this verse as the hinge on which the next turns: two sons bearing the names of God's lordship and fatherhood, holding their father's office at the far edge of the land, out of his sight though never out of God's — a warning, Henry concludes, to depend not on education or reputation but on divine grace.
3:
The indictment of Samuel's sons is short, and the witnesses weigh every word of it. Clarke finds their iniquity 'pointed out in three words': they turned aside after lucre — the Hebrew batsa signifying to cut or clip, so that Parkhurst likened it to the clipping of coin, avarice 'getting money by hook or by crook'; they took bribes, shochad, gifts or presents 'to blind their eyes'; and they perverted judgment, turning it out of its regular path and selling it to the highest bidder, so that the wicked rich man carried his cause and the poor man was oppressed. Clarke cannot resist his own nation's history, recalling how justice was bought and delayed in the king's court until Magna Charta promised, 'To no man will we sell, to no man will we deny or defer, justice and right.' The Targum, cited by Gill and Clarke alike, says they looked after 'the mammon of unrighteousness'; and Matthew Henry presses the apostolic verdict that the love of money is the root of all evil — 'pernicious in any, but especially in judges.' Samuel had taken no bribes (1 Samuel 12:3), but his sons had, and 'enquired who bid highest, not who had right on his side.'

Gill guards the phrase 'walked not in his ways' from a trivial reading: it does not mean merely that they failed to ride their father's circuit, as some Jewish writers took it, but that they walked not in the fear of God, in the paths of religion and righteousness. Ellicott observes that this sin, 'at all times a fatally common one in the East,' was expressly denounced in the Law (Exodus 23:6–8; Deuteronomy 16:19), and marks the unnerving parallel: the same ills that ruined Eli's house now threatened Samuel's. Wesley explains how the corruption had lain hidden: 'Opportunity and temptation discovered that corruption in them which till now was hid from their father' — and adds the sorrowful commonplace that the sons of eminently good men have often been eminently wicked.

Yet the prophet-judge, Ellicott insists, acted differently from the high-priestly judge. Where Eli indulged his sons, Samuel — by his own part in procuring the election of Saul — saw them quickly deposed from authority; and the discipline seems to have borne fruit, for in after days Heman, Samuel's grandson, stood as the king's seer at the head of David's choir in the house of God (1 Chronicles 6:33; 1 Chronicles 25:4–5). Henry's caution gathers up the whole: those that have the most grace themselves cannot give grace to their children; let none be secure either of themselves or theirs, but depend on divine grace.
4:
The gathering at Ramah strikes the witnesses as both constitutionally orderly and historically momentous. Gill identifies the elders as 'the heads of the tribes, and fathers of the houses and families of Israel, the principal persons of age and authority,' assembling from some appointed place of rendezvous. Ellicott sees here 'a clear trace of a popular assembly which seems in all times to have existed in Israel' — a representative council of elders, qualified by birth or office, that had met for deliberation even during the Egyptian bondage (Exodus 3:16), and to which Ewald even found allusions in Psalms 82. The demand for a king, in other words, did not come from a mob but from Israel's ancient parliament.

Matthew Henry feels the shock of the moment: 'the starting of a matter perfectly new and surprising,' the setting up of kingly government in Israel. The idea may long have been talked of among those 'given to change,' but it had never till now been publicly proposed; Abimelech had been 'little better than a titular king,' though said to reign over Israel (Judges 9:22), and his fall had perhaps made the very title odious. Yet Henry is careful to credit the elders' manner: they gathered by common consent, 'not in a riotous tumultuous manner, but with the respect due to his character,' coming to the prophet's own house at Ramah with a formal address. The revolution, when it came, came constitutionally, through the front door.

Keil and Delitzsch supply the observation that governs the whole chapter's interpretation: the elders' request 'resembles so completely the law of the king in Deuteronomy 17:14' — down to the very phrase 'like all the nations' — 'that the distinct allusion to it is unmistakeable.' The elders, on this reading, 'simply desired what Jehovah had foretold through His servant Moses, as a thing that would take place in the future and for which He had even made provision.' Samuel's age and the degeneracy of his sons furnished the occasion; the Torah itself seemed to furnish the warrant. That is what makes the episode so searching for every reader: a request framed in the words of Scripture, presented decently and in order by lawful representatives, can still — as the following verses reveal — carry a heart that has quietly rejected God. The witnesses thus prepare us to distinguish, with the narrative itself, between the form of the petition and the spirit of the petitioners.
5:
The elders ground their petition on two facts — 'thou art old, and thy sons walk not in thy ways' — and the witnesses probe both the truth and the unkindness of the plea. Gill allows the facts but not the inference: Samuel's age 'was no reproach to him, nor was it becoming them to upbraid him with it'; having gained experience with age, he was rather more fit for the office, though he could no longer ride his circuits. Matthew Henry is sharper: it was 'unkind, ungrateful, nay, and unjust, to cast him off when he was old, who had spent his days in doing them good' — old age 'should have been counted worthy of double honour,' and a small thing will serve factious spirits for a colour to desire a change. As for the sons, Henry observes that Samuel, unlike Eli, had not indulged them; had the elders simply proved the charge of bribery, 'he would have superseded their commissions and punished them. But this would not content the elders of Israel; they had another project in their head.'

Wesley presses the logical gap: 'their desires exceed their reasons, which extended no farther than to the removal of Samuel's sons.' Nor would the remedy cure the disease, but aggravate it — 'for the sons of their king were likely to be as corrupt as Samuel's sons; and, if they were, would not be so easily removed.' A hereditary crown entrenches the very evil an elective judgeship could correct. And to the plea 'like all the nations' Wesley answers that 'there was not the like reason,' since God had separated Israel from all other nations and taken them into his own immediate government. Clarke agrees on what was really being surrendered: hitherto Israel had lived under a theocracy, with 'no other king but God'; what they chiefly wanted was a visible general to lead them in battle.

Yet Ellicott asks us to notice something honourable hidden in the address: what confidence these elders must have reposed in their aged judge, to urge upon him, to his face, 'his own growing infirmity and the unworthiness of his own sons, whom he had himself appointed'! They knew the man of God would give them the wisest counsel 'utterly regardless of any private interest or feeling' — and, he adds, the result justified their perfect confidence. Keil and Delitzsch again point to the Deuteronomic frame: the petition was couched almost in the terms of Deuteronomy 17:14 itself. The verse thus holds together a lawful form, a plausible occasion, and — as God himself will presently disclose — a faithless motive.
6:
Why was Samuel displeased? The witnesses answer with one voice on the negative: not at the personal slight. Gill says it was 'not that they called him an old man... nor for observing the unbecoming walk of his sons.' Keil and Delitzsch find that reading in the grammar itself: the clause 'when they said, Give us a king to judge us' explains and fixes the object of his displeasure, precluding any reference to his age or his sons. Clarke names the true grievance: Samuel saw that the demand 'amounted to a formal renunciation of the Divine government.' Wesley fills in the moral anatomy — God was dishonoured by the distrust, the ambition, and the 'itch after changes' which were the manifest causes of the desire, and Samuel foresaw the misery the people would bring upon themselves.

But the witnesses divide over the request itself. Ellicott argues that it was 'perfectly justifiable in the elders' to come to this resolution: the directions of Deuteronomy 17:14–20 are clear and explicit, Moses had evidently looked forward to the change, and no distant period was contemplated. Keil and Delitzsch agree that Samuel cannot have regarded the monarchy as irreconcilable with God's sovereignty, or even as untimely — else he would simply have refused the request as ungodly, instead of laying it before the Lord. On the other side, Clarke's 'formal renunciation' and Wesley's catalogue of corrupt motives treat the petition as sin, whatever provision the Law had made; and Matthew Henry holds that God designed them a king, a man after his own heart, when Samuel was dead, 'but they would anticipate God's counsel.' The reconciliation most of them gesture toward is that the thing asked was lawful, but the spirit and season of the asking were faithless.

All are agreed on what Samuel did with his wound. 'It cut him to the heart,' says Henry, 'and it drove him to his knees': he gave them no answer for the present, but 'prayed unto the Lord,' spreading the case before God and so making himself easy — the pattern of Philippians 4:6. Wesley adds that he prayed both 'for the pardon of their sin' and for direction. Ellicott, most tenderly of all, imagines what the moment cost: though Moses had foreseen and even arranged for the change, 'to one like Samuel it was very bitter' to give up for ever 'the splendid Hebrew ideal that his people were the subjects of the Eternal King, ruled directly by Him.' The old man's greatness appears precisely here — displeased, he does not defend himself; he carries the matter to his King.
7:
God's answer to Samuel's prayer is at once a command, a consolation, and a wound. Gill notes the mode — a vision, or an articulate voice — but all the witnesses dwell on the substance: 'they have not rejected thee, but they have rejected me.' Clarke states it plainly: 'They wish to put that government in the hands of a mortal, which was always in the hands of their God.' Wesley reads the divine arithmetic of the insult — 'this injury and contumely reflects chiefly upon me and my government' — and observes that God grants their desire 'in anger, and for their punishment.' Clarke coins the maxim the others echo: 'So we find God grants that in his displeasure which he withholds in his mercy.'

Matthew Henry draws out the consolation for every servant of God who is slighted: 'I share with thee in the affront.' If God interests himself in the indignities done to us, we may well afford to bear them patiently. And he explains what precisely was being refused: God reigns over all the world, but Israel's government had been 'in a more peculiar manner than ever any government was, a Theocracy' — their judges called immediately by God, their national affairs conducted by Thus saith the Lord. This, Henry shrewdly suggests, was exactly what wearied them: under such a government 'they could not transgress at so cheap a rate as other nations could,' and perhaps that was the true reason they desired to stand upon other nations' terms with God.

No witness enters this verse more feelingly than Ellicott, who calls the words spoken to Samuel 'very touching and very sad.' Touching, in their extreme tenderness to the noble old man: take courage, they seem to say — this complaint 'is directed not against you, the judge, but against Me, the invisible King.' Sad, because they record 'the deliberate abandonment by the Eternal God of His first intention as regarded Israel — the deliberate lowering of the grand ideal once formed for His chosen people.' Here, he says, 'a corner of the veil which hangs between the creature and the Creator is lifted for a moment': we see how sadly possible it is for man, in the exercise of his freedom, to mar the glorious work arranged for him by his God, and we catch sight of the sorrowful regret — 'if we may use the term' — of the Creator over the perverse folly of his creatures. Yet even so, the command stands: 'Hearken unto the voice of the people.' Judgment and mercy travel together in the concession — God will give them their king, and will overrule even their rebellion for the bringing in, at last, of the King after his own heart.
8:
God sets the elders' demand in the long perspective of the nation's whole history: 'according to all the works which they have done since the day that I brought them up out of Egypt... so do they also unto thee.' Gill hears in this the divine verdict that the request 'was no new thing': all that Israel had done since they were so wonderfully brought out of Egyptian bondage had been 'all of a piece with this; one continued series of ingratitude, of rebellion against God, and against his servants that he employed under him, as Moses, Aaron' and the rest. The sin of Ramah is not an aberration but the latest link in a chain reaching back to the wilderness — the golden calf, the murmurings, the mutinies against Moses — and the God who has watched it all is neither surprised nor overthrown by it.

Wesley catches the pastoral intent of the sentence: it is spoken 'for Samuel's comfort and vindication' — as if God said to his old servant, 'Thou farest no worse than myself.' The prophet who feels himself cast off after a lifetime of service is being folded into a fellowship of rejection with the Lord he serves. Matthew Henry develops the same thought: Samuel 'must not think it strange, nor marvel at the matter, for they do as they always have done.' The people had of late been so respectful and obsequious to Samuel that he began to hope 'they were cured of their old stubborn disposition; but now he found himself deceived in them.' They had always been rude to their governors — witness Moses and Aaron — and, more than that, 'they have forsaken me and served other gods': the greatness of their crime in affecting new gods, Henry remarks, 'may make this crime of affecting new governors seem little.' He seals it with the prophets' own language: they were 'called transgressors from the womb' (Isaiah 48:8), and this had been their manner from their youth up (Jeremiah 22:21).

The verse thus becomes, in the witnesses' hands, a small theology of ministerial discouragement. The servant of God who is slighted stands where Moses, Aaron, and God himself have stood before him; ingratitude toward God's instruments is finally ingratitude toward God; and the long-suffering of the Lord — who has borne this 'from the day I brought them up out of Egypt even unto this day' and still answers, still provides, still governs — is the pattern for his servants' patience. Samuel is not being told that the wound is imaginary, but that it is shared, ancient, and known; and that is enough to send him back to the people with a message rather than a resignation.
9:
The command is double: hearken to their voice, yet 'protest solemnly unto them, and shew them the manner of the king.' Keil and Delitzsch weigh the Hebrew: the phrase means to bear witness against a person, 'to point out to him his wrong' — neither merely 'warn them earnestly,' as De Wette rendered it, nor 'solemnly expound,' with Thenius. Samuel is to stand as a witness in God's court, entering the people's folly upon the record before it is committed. Wesley hears the mercy inside the protest: it is made 'that, if it be possible, thou mayst yet prevent their sin and misery.' Even in the act of granting, God is still dissuading.

What, then, is 'the manner of the king'? Clarke is emphatic that mishpat here 'signifies simply what the king would and might require, according to the manner in which kings in general ruled; all of whom, in those times, were absolute and despotic.' He quotes Puffendorf's paraphrase at length — the splendid attendance, the standing forces, the daughters in court service, the salaried ministers, the tenth exacted of all — and then turns his guns on Grotius: it is 'perfectly vain' to argue that the passage states what a king, as king, may anywhere claim by virtue of his office. 'This was the manner of Saul, but Saul was not a king of God's choosing: He gave him in his wrath, and took him away in his displeasure' (Hosea 13:11); the manner of the king of God's choice is distinctly detailed in Deuteronomy 17:15–20, and no potentate who affects to rule by divine right may arrogate the tyrant's charter from this chapter. Matthew Henry reads it the same way: Samuel speaks not of 'a just and honest right of a king,' but of 'such a right as the kings of the nations had then acquired.'

Henry also ponders why God should grant what he condemns: sometimes 'he crosses us in love,' and at other times 'he gratifies us in wrath' — as he gave them quails (Psalms 106:15). The concession would let them be 'beaten with their own rod,' would forestall something worse (open rebellion, or wholesale apostasy to the nations' gods), and would serve God's own wise purposes even through their foolish counsels. Gill keeps the sequence simple: hearken, and appoint them a king as they desire — but only after the solemn testimony has been given, so that Israel can never say it was not warned. The verse leaves us with a God who honours human freedom even in its folly, yet insists that the folly go into the record first.
10:
Samuel's fidelity as a messenger is the first thing the witnesses mark. Keil and Delitzsch note that, in accordance with his instructions, he told the people all the words of Jehovah — all that God had said to him in vv.7–9 — and then proclaimed the right of the king. Gill spells out what that report contained: how God considered their request 'as a rejection of him as their king,' how it was of a piece with all their old ingratitude, and how, since they were so importunate, it should nevertheless be granted — but with all the inconveniences and evils that would follow laid honestly before them. Nothing softened, nothing suppressed: the prophet delivers the rebuke, the concession, and the warning entire, to the very people 'that asked of him a king.' It is the pattern of every faithful ministry — to tell all the words of the Lord, the unwelcome with the welcome, to the very persons whose desires they cross.

Matthew Henry draws from the catalogue that follows a distinction that has become proverbial: 'The law of God and the manner of men widely differ from each other; the former should be our rule in the several relations of life; the latter should be the measure of our expectations from others.' What Samuel is about to describe is not how a king ought to govern — Deuteronomy 17:15–20 had settled that — but how kings such as the nations bred would in fact behave; and a people choosing a government must reckon on men as they are, not as they should be. Henry widens the application to the spiritual realm: 'Those that submit to the government of the world and the flesh are told plainly what hard masters they are, and what tyranny the dominion of sin is' — and yet, like Israel, they will exchange God's government for it.

From the same passage Henry distils counsel for subjects of every age: since the people were obstinate and would have their king, 'our wisdom is to be thankful for the advantages, and patient under the disadvantages, of the government we may live under; and to pray continually for our rulers,' that we may live under them in all godliness and honesty (1 Timothy 2:2). And he adds the gentler test of a right heart: 'It is a hopeful symptom when our desires of worldly objects can brook delay,' and when we can refer the time and manner of their granting to God's providence — precisely what Israel, in its haste, could not do. The verse thus stands between the divine sentence and the human refusal: God has spoken all, Samuel has told all, and the responsibility now passes wholly to the hearers.
11:
'This will be the manner of the king that shall reign over you: he will take your sons.' Gill fixes the sense of 'manner' at the outset: 'not in which he ought to proceed, but what he will do' — and not the manner of one king only, but of kings in general, 'who are commonly inclined to arbitrary power.' He reaches for Aristotle to say why: he that would have the mind or reason preside 'would have God and the laws rule; but he that would have a man to reign, adds also a lust' — the philosopher and the prophet agreeing that to enthrone a man is to enthrone his appetites with him. Wesley compresses the whole oracle into two words on 'he will take': 'injuriously and by violence.'

Keil and Delitzsch attend to the details of the picture: the king will make the people's sons 'his retainers at court, his charioteers, riders, and runners.' They defend the Hebrew text against Thenius's proposed correction from the Septuagint: the singular suffix should stand, for the word denotes 'not war-chariots, but the king's state-carriage,' and the accompanying term means 'a saddle-horse' rather than a rider. The first exaction, in other words, is not even military necessity but courtly display — free-born sons running before one man's carriage. Matthew Henry saw the same point: a multitude of attendants is 'the chief grandeur of princes, and the imaginary glory of great men,' and it must be fed with other men's children.

Ellicott hears in the speech a statesman's question rather than a scolding: Samuel, 'without blaming the people for their desire, quietly asks them if they were in real earnest' — whether they had counted the constitutional cost. Were they willing to exchange their old freedom 'for the condition of subjection to a sovereign who, after the manner of those other kings — the Pharaohs, for instance — would of course govern Israel after his own will?' — in his blunt summary, 'to give up their Republic for a Despotism?' He paints the change of life to come: a gorgeous court such as some of them had seen on the banks of the Nile or the Euphrates; the best and choicest of Israel summoned there; the old pastoral life under vine and fig-tree giving way to the pleasures and vices of a brilliant city, and war on a scale they had never dreamed of. 'Had they thought of all this when they asked for a king?' The verse begins the long ledger of 'he will take' — the witnesses agree it is the abiding portrait of what every idolised power, political or otherwise, does with those who insist upon it.
12:
The catalogue proceeds from the court to the army and the royal estates: captains over thousands and captains over fifties, ploughmen for his ground, reapers for his harvest, smiths for his weapons and the furniture of his chariots. Keil and Delitzsch observe that the greatest and the smallest military commissions are named so as to comprehend everything between them — the whole gradation of officers and soldiers is meant — and that the clauses all still hang on that one governing verb of v.11, 'he will take.' Ellicott adds the philologist's note that has charmed English readers: 'to ear his ground' is simply the old English word for ploughing, the Anglo-Saxon earian, cousin to the Latin arare.

Might not commissions and captaincies be an honour rather than a burden? The witnesses answer with a common realism. Gill grants they are 'posts of honour, yet when they are not matter of choice, and especially being precarious, and depending on the arbitrary will of a prince, are not eligible' — least of all to men who had chosen another sort of life. Wesley goes further: the Hebrew says the king appoints them 'to, or for himself — for his own fancy, or glory, and not only when the necessities of the kingdom require it'; and even in the promoted, the honour would be 'accompanied with great dangers, and pernicious snares of many kinds, which those faint shadows of glory could not recompense.' Meanwhile the public would groan under the pomp, their lands and fruits taken to support the state of the new grandees. As for the forced husbandry, Wesley notes the king 'will press them for all sorts of his work, and that upon his own terms' — and at seasons, perhaps, 'when possibly their own fields required all their time and pains.'

Matthew Henry feels the domestic revolution compressed into the verse: 'those that used to work for their parents and themselves must work for him, ear his ground, and reap his harvest — and count it their preferment too.' That last clause carries his irony: despotism not only takes; it teaches its subjects to call the taking a favour. So the witnesses read the verse as the second column in Samuel's ledger — after the sons taken for show come the sons taken for war and for the royal demesne — and they leave the reader with the standing question of the whole oracle: whatever is set up in God's place will conscript, by degrees, the labour, the seasons, and at last the self-understanding of those who demanded it.
13:
Now the ledger reaches the daughters: 'he will take your daughters to be confectionaries, and to be cooks, and to be bakers.' The witnesses first settle what the first trade was. Gill explains that confectionaries 'deal in spices, and mix them, and make them up in various forms very agreeable to the taste,' remarking that though in his own country and times such arts belonged to men, in those times and places they were the business of women; some versions, he adds, render the word unguentariae — makers or sellers of ointments — a trade known in the classical world, 'such was Lydia in Juvenal.' Ellicott agrees the word is 'better rendered perfumers — that is, makers of ointments and scents, of which Orientals are inordinately fond,' and Keil and Delitzsch translate the trio 'preparers of ointments, cooks, and bakers' for the royal court. The picture is of a palace kitchen and stillroom staffed by requisition.

The exaction may look lighter than the conscription of sons for war, but Wesley judges it heavier: the taking of daughters 'would be more grievous to their parents, and more dangerous to themselves, because of the tenderness of that sex, and their liableness to many injuries.' A son pressed into the king's fields keeps at least his hardihood; a daughter carried into an eastern court is exposed to what her father can neither see nor prevent. Matthew Henry gives the family's-eye view: the king 'will take your daughters, the most ingenious and handy of them, whom you hoped to prefer to houses and tables of their own; and, whether you be willing or no, they must be his confectioners, and cooks, and bakers.' The daughters bred for their own households become fixtures of another man's luxury.

Taken together, the witnesses find in the verse a deliberate stroke in Samuel's argument. The despot's appetite is not confined to what a state plausibly needs — soldiers, revenues, lands — but extends to delicacies: perfumes, pastries, sauces. Henry had already noted that such a king 'will not be content to dine with his neighbours upon a sacrifice,' as Samuel did (1 Samuel 9:13), but must have dainty dishes and delicate sauces, and other men's daughters to make them. Luxury, once enthroned, is as coercive as war; the same verb — 'he will take' — governs the perfumer's vial as the captain's commission. A people that would not spare its daughters to serve the tabernacle of God will find them requisitioned for the kitchens of the king it preferred.
14:
'He will take your fields, and your vineyards, and your oliveyards, even the best of them, and give them to his servants.' Here the witnesses genuinely part company over what exactly the king seizes. Gill argues that the verse intends 'the whole increase of their land, their corn, and wine, and oil' — the fruits of fields, vineyards, and oliveyards rather than the freeholds themselves. His proof is the story of Naboth: kings 'might not, and did not by their absolute authority, take away those from their subjects; otherwise Ahab would have taken away Naboth's vineyard at once, nor would Jezebel have needed to have taken such a method as she did' (1 Kings 21). Even Israel's worst king had to stage a judicial murder to get one vineyard — evidence, for Gill, that outright confiscation was never the acknowledged royal right.

Wesley reads the same words, and the same story, the other way: the king will take 'your fields — by fraud or force, as Ahab did from Naboth,' and 'will not only take the fruits of your lands for his own use, but will take away your possessions to give to his servants.' What Gill cites as proof that kings could not seize estates, Wesley cites as proof that they did — law or no law. Keil and Delitzsch side in substance with Wesley: 'all their possessions he would also take to himself: the good (i.e., the best) fields, vineyards, and olive-gardens he would take away, and give to his servants,' the tithing of produce being a distinct exaction in the following verse. They add two notes of scholarship: the 'servants' include the sarisim, literally eunuchs, used here in the wider sense of royal chamberlains; and they flag the textual difficulty in v.16 that the Septuagint helps to resolve.

Matthew Henry feels where the barb of the verse lies — not merely in the taking but in the giving. The king will seize the inheritances 'which descended to you from your ancestors, and which you hoped to leave to your posterity,' and will not only take them to himself — 'you could bear that better' — 'but he will give them to his servants, who will be your masters, and bear rule over that for which you have laboured. How will you like that?' Favourites must be ennobled, and ennobled favourites must be endowed, and the endowments can come only out of the people's patrimony. Whether the verse means produce or possession, all the witnesses agree on its function in Samuel's protest: under the king Israel demands, no man's ancestral ground will be securely his own.
15:
From lands the oracle turns to revenue: 'he will take the tenth of your seed, and of your vineyards, and give to his officers, and to his servants.' Gill notes the timing of the exaction — the tenth taken 'when grown up and ripe, as their wheat and barley': the tax-gatherer stands at the threshing-floor and the winepress, claiming his portion at the very moment of harvest. Keil and Delitzsch read the verse within the sweep of the whole passage as 'a fearful picture of the tyranny of the king,' drawn — they insist against the critics — 'from the despotic conduct of the heathen kings,' and not presupposing Israel's own later, painful experience of the monarchy; Samuel needed no history of Solomon to describe what every eastern throne already exhibited.

Wesley sets the royal tenth beside the sacred one: 'Besides the several tenths which God hath reserved for his service, he will, when he pleaseth, impose another tenth upon you.' The king's tithe does not replace God's; it is added to it — a second lord claiming a lord's portion. Matthew Henry had made the same comparison cut deeper: 'You think the tenths, the double tenths, which the law of God has appointed for the support of the church, grievous enough, and grudge the payment of them; but, if you have a king, there must issue another tenth out of your estates, which will be levied with more rigour.' His counsel to the elders is a tradesman's: 'Consider the expense with the magnificence, and whether it will quit cost.' A people too thrifty for God's tithe will find themselves paying a heavier one to Caesar, and collected less gently.

On the recipients of the levy Wesley finds a darker hint. 'Officers' is in the Hebrew his eunuchs — 'which may imply a farther injury, that he should, against the command of God, make some of his people eunuchs; and take those into his court and favour which God would have cast out of the congregation.' The tithe of Israel's fields would maintain a court whose very structure defied Israel's law. Keil and Delitzsch soften the word to 'courtiers and servants' in its wider usage, but the moral geography is the same in all the witnesses: the produce of covenant land, which the Law had directed toward the Levite, the stranger, the fatherless, and the widow, would be re-routed to sustain a palace establishment. What God's tenth built up — worship, mercy, and the common life — the king's tenth would spend on grandeur; and Israel, having grudged the one, would groan under the other.
16:
'He will take your menservants, and your maidservants, and your goodliest young men, and your asses, and put them to his work.' Gill takes the plain sense: the king will draft household servants 'into his own family, for his own use and service, if he wants them, or likes them better than what he has' — even a man's domestic establishment is subject to the royal eye and the royal preference. Wesley, as throughout the catalogue, glosses the verb: he will take them 'by constraint, and without sufficient recompense.' Requisition, not hire, is the manner of the king.

But the phrase 'your goodliest young men' provokes a genuine text-critical debate among the witnesses. Ellicott reports that the Septuagint here reads 'your best oxen,' a rendering that 'required only the change of one letter of similar sound in the Hebrew word,' and he judges boldly: 'This was, no doubt, the reading of the original text, as the young men seem included among the sons in vv.11–12, and oxen would naturally precede the asses mentioned in the next clause.' Keil and Delitzsch reach the same verdict with full apparatus: the word for 'young men,' standing between the household slaves and the asses, is 'very striking and altogether unsuitable,' and 'in all probability it is only an ancient copyist's error' for 'your oxen,' as the Greek ta boukolia shows; menservants and maidservants then answer to oxen and asses, persons to beasts of burden, while the young men remain where the oracle had already placed them, among the conscripted sons. Gill and Wesley, on the other hand, expound the received text without misgiving, letting the 'goodliest young men' stand as one more class of the taken.

Either way, the witnesses agree on the force of the verse in Samuel's protest. The inventory has now descended from sons and daughters through fields and tithes to servants and draught-animals: nothing in an Israelite's household, from his firstborn to his ass, lies outside the reach of 'he will take.' The very structure of the sentence — persons and beasts strung together in one clause, all 'put to his work' — enacts the degradation it describes: under the king Israel demands, the line between a subject's family and a subject's livestock blurs, for both alike are inventory to the crown. It is the penultimate entry in the ledger, and it prepares the climactic sentence of v.17: 'and ye shall be his servants.' The people who would not have God's gentle reign will find that their goods were only the down-payment; the final levy is themselves.
17:
The catalogue closes with a levy and a sentence: 'He will take the tenth of your sheep: and ye shall be his servants.' Gill extends the tithe to its full breadth — not the flocks only 'but of their herds also, which are here included, as Kimchi observes': the royal tenth, already laid on seed and vineyard, now reaches every animal in the fold and stall. But it is the closing clause that carries the weight, and Ellicott states its function precisely: 'This statement generally includes all that has gone before. In other words, Ye elders and chiefs of the people must make up your minds, in the event of electing a king, to the loss of all political and social freedom.' Every particular exaction — sons, daughters, fields, tithes, servants, beasts — is gathered into one final predicate: ye shall be his. Wesley puts it without varnish: 'he will use you like slaves, and deprive you of that liberty which now you enjoy.' The nation that asked for a master would get one.

Ellicott then lets history preach the sermon Samuel's hearers refused. How bitterly Israel felt the royal yoke, 'even in the successful and glorious reign of King Solomon,' the sequel showed: when the great king died, the public discontent at his brilliant but despotic rule broke out in the cry of 1 Kings 12:4 — 'thy father made our yoke grievous' — and the revolution that followed split the people into two nations. The prophecy of their last judge was vindicated within three reigns, and by the very dynasty of their greatest kings. Ellicott also quotes the Speaker's Commentary for a critical observation with apologetic force: 'This whole passage bears internal evidence of having been written before the establishment of the monarchy' — the warning is genuine prediction, not a chronicler's hindsight dressed as prophecy.

Read whole, the verse is the hinge between warning and choice. Samuel has finished the ledger; nothing remains to disclose. The witnesses observe how the oracle's grammar has shifted across seven verses: it began with what the king will take, and it ends with what the people will be. That is, for them, the anatomy of every surrender to a lord other than God — the exactions are itemised first, but the last and greatest forfeit is the self. Israel had been brought out of the house of bondage (Exodus 20:2) by a God who asked tithes and got grudging; now, weary of his light yoke, they are told in so many words that the alternative is servitude — and, as the next verse shows, they will choose it with their eyes open.
18:
The oracle ends beyond the exactions, in the years of regret: 'And ye shall cry out in that day because of your king which ye shall have chosen you; and the Lord will not hear you in that day.' Gill pictures the cry — groaning under the king's 'power and pride, his oppression and tyranny, his heavy exactions, and intolerable yoke,' yet unable to shake them off; all they could do would be to cry out, 'and which would be the more aggravated, because they brought all this upon themselves.' The chosen king is the unremovable king: a judge's commission could be revoked, but a crown, once granted, holds. Wesley hears the terrible symmetry in the refusal of heaven: 'Ye shall bitterly mourn for the sad effects of this inordinate desire' — and God 'will not hear, because you will not hear, nor obey his counsel in this day.' The deafness of 'that day' answers the deafness of this one.

Matthew Henry presses the sting that they will have 'none but God to complain to.' Once, he notes, the people did complain to the prince himself, and were answered 'according to the manner of the king': 'Your yoke is heavy, and I will add to it' (1 Kings 12:11). And when they turn at last to God, they cannot expect a hearing, both because they had been deaf to his admonitions and because this trouble in particular was of their own procuring. He draws the general rule with characteristic edge: 'When we bring ourselves into distress by our own irregular desires and projects, we justly forfeit the comfort of prayer and the benefit of divine aids.' Self-willed choices carry their consequences past the reach of complaint, though not — the whole of Scripture adds — past the reach of repentance.

Ellicott supplies the historical fulfilment in its darkest colours. After the kingdom split, a large proportion of the northern kings 'fulfilled in their lives and government the dark forebodings of the seer': the north broke with ark and temple, set up a semi-idolatrous worship, and lay under sovereigns unswayed by any holy influence. 'The lives of the Israelites who still loved the law of the Lord must have been very bitter and hard when men like Omri and Ahab reigned with all their cruel power in Tirzah and Samaria. But no prayers then availed; one wicked dynasty succeeded another, until the cup of iniquity was filled, and Israel carried away captive for ever out of their fair land.' The verse thus stands as the most solemn line of Samuel's protest: God may grant a demanded idol, and then leave the demanders to live with it — a warning the witnesses apply to every prayer that will not take heaven's no for an answer.
19:
'Nevertheless the people refused to obey the voice of Samuel; and they said, Nay; but we will have a king over us.' Keil and Delitzsch register the astonishment the narrative intends: Samuel had pointed out the dangers of the monarchy 'in so alarming a manner, that they ought to have been brought to reflection, and to have desisted from their demand' — but the people refused to hearken, and simply repeated it. Adam Clarke compresses the perversity into a phrase: 'They would have the king, his manner and all, notwithstanding the solemn warning which they here receive.' They did not dispute the forecast; they accepted the tyranny in advance, as the price of the pageant. Gill notes what exactly was being refused: the advice 'not to think of a king, but be content with the government under which they were' — contentment being precisely the grace they lacked.

Ellicott, ever attentive to the constitutional machinery, will not let us imagine a mob shouting Samuel down: 'The warning words of the prophet-judge were evidently carefully considered and debated in a formal assembly, but the majority at least abided by the terms of their request.' The decision was deliberate, parliamentary, and therefore the more culpable: Israel voted for the yoke. Matthew Henry hears the coarsening of the language between the first petition and this reply: 'Before it was, Pray, make us a king; now it is, Nay, but we will have a king; yea, that we will, because we will.' They could not answer Samuel's arguments nor deny their force, 'and yet they grow more violent in their request, and more insolent' — and he draws the maxim: 'See the absurdity of inordinate desires, and how they rob men of their reason.'

Henry adds the observation that turns the verse from folly to tragedy: they could not stay God's time. God had intimated in the law that Israel should in due time have a king (Deuteronomy 17:14–15); 'could they but have waited ten or twelve years longer they would have had David, a king of God's giving in mercy, and all the calamities that attended the setting up of Saul would have been prevented.' The demand was not for a wrong thing but for the right thing wrenched out of season — and 'sudden resolves and hasty desires make work for a long and leisurely repentance.' In that sentence the witnesses find the verse's abiding pastoral use: the difference between faith and self-will is often nothing but the willingness to wait, and Israel's 'Nay; but we will' is the perennial voice of the heart that has ceased to ask and begun to insist.
20:
The people now state their whole creed in one sentence: 'that we also may be like all the nations; and that our king may judge us, and go out before us, and fight our battles.' Clarke parses the three offices demanded: 'may judge us' — which 'appears to be a rejection of Samuel'; 'go out before us' — 'be in every respect our head and governor'; 'and fight our battles' — 'be the general of our armies.' Judge, head, and general: everything God had been to them, transferred to a man. On the opening clause the witnesses can scarcely contain themselves. Gill: a king they would have 'even though they were slaves, like them... such was their stupidity,' for 'it was their greatest honour and glory, as well as happiness, not to be like other nations' — in religion, laws, liberties, and above all in having God for King in a sense no other people had. Wesley bursts out, 'What stupidity!', citing the oracles that had made unlikeness Israel's crown — 'the people shall dwell alone' (Numbers 23:9), 'Israel then shall dwell in safety alone' (Deuteronomy 33:28).

Ellicott hears in the words something 'strangely painful': the elders 'forgot, or chose to ignore, the solitary position of lofty pre-eminence God had given them among the nations,' and this haste 'to descend from the pedestal on which their God had set them was in the eye of one like Samuel a strange inexplicable foolishness.' The chosen people's deepest temptation, on this reading, is not idolatry in the gross but conformity — the longing to be ordinary, to trade election for resemblance.

On the military plea the witnesses answer from the chapter before. Matthew Henry asks the withering question: 'Could they ever desire a battle better fought for them than the last was, by Samuel's prayer and God's thunder?' (1 Samuel 7:10). Was victory by such means too sure for them — were they 'fond of trying the chance of war at the same uncertainty that others did'? He follows the desire to its bitter fulfilment: 'Their first king was slain in a battle, which none of their judges ever were; so was Josiah, one of the last and best.' The arm of flesh they demanded fell on Gilboa, while no judge who leaned on the Lord had ever so fallen. Keil and Delitzsch keep the record plain — the demand was repeated with its reasons, and Samuel carried it to God — but the theological verdict is common property among the witnesses: Israel here exchanged the God who fought for them for a man who would fight before them, and counted the exchange a bargain. It is the old bad trade of sight for faith, and the witnesses commend the verse to every generation tempted to think God's invisible government insufficient armour against visible enemies.
21:
'And Samuel heard all the words of the people, and he rehearsed them in the ears of the Lord.' The witnesses linger first over the hearing. Gill notes that Samuel heard 'patiently, and without interruption; attentively heard them, took notice of them, laid them up in his memory; but gave no answer to them' — the old prophet neither argues further nor storms out, but receives the people's final word entire, that he may report it entire. The scene of the reporting divides gently among them. Clarke supposes that 'he went to the altar, and in his secret devotion laid the whole business before God.' Ellicott paints it more fully: the seer returns from the council chamber 'to some quiet spot, probably the sanctuary he had set up in his own Ramah of the Watchers, where he poured out his heart before his God-Friend.' Wesley attends to the purpose: he repeated the words 'privately between God and himself; for his own vindication and comfort: and as a foundation for his prayers to God, for direction and assistance.'

Matthew Henry asks the obvious question and answers it richly: why rehearse to God what God perfectly knew? Not for God's information, 'but thus he dealt faithfully between God and Israel, as a prophet, returning the answer to him that sent him; and thus he waited on God for further direction.' God 'is fully acquainted with the state of the case we are in care and doubt about, but he will know it from us.' And Henry finds in the phrase itself a picture of privileged intimacy: the rehearsing 'in the ears of the Lord' was done in private — for the people were in no mind to join him in prayer — and 'it bespeaks a holy familiarity, to which God graciously admits his people: they speak in the ears of the Lord, as one friend whispers with another.' Such communion, he adds, is 'meat they have to eat which the world knows not of' (John 4:32).

Between the people's ultimatum and God's final answer, then, stands a praying man. The witnesses draw the portrait deliberately: Samuel is the wronged party, the deposed ruler, the slighted prophet — and his whole recourse is to tell God about it. He does not edit the people's words to their disadvantage; he 'rehearses' them, faithfully, as an ambassador returns a message. In him they see the model for every servant of God caught between an immovable people and a sovereign Lord: hear all, answer nothing in haste, and carry the whole matter — insult, anxiety, and all — into the ears of the Friend who hears. What Samuel received there, the next verse tells.
22:
The chapter closes with the divine fiat and a quiet dispersal: 'Hearken unto their voice, and make them a king... Go ye every man unto his city.' Ellicott observes that this is the third time (vv.7, 9) the Eternal has used the self-same words to his reluctant servant — the remonstrance was permitted, its outcome known from the first — and he dwells on 'the perfect trust' God placed in Samuel, who was 'entrusted with all the arrangements connected with this vital change in the Hebrew constitution, although his own downfall from power was necessarily involved in it.' The people shared the confidence: having heard their judge's warnings, debated, and reiterated their request, the elders — a truly representative body, as 'every man unto his city' shows — 'dispersed quietly, each one journeying to his own city,' ready to depose him yet waiting for his sign. Wesley paraphrases the dismissal: 'Betake yourselves to your several occasions, till you hear more from me in this matter'; and Matthew Henry explains the delay — 'the designation of the person must be left to God'; the people had now no more to do but keep the peace and expect the issue.

Clarke will not leave the chapter without a reckoning, and draws up his conclusions like articles: it was not God who changed Israel's government, but the people themselves; God permitted the king but did not approve him; he 'never gave up the supreme government,' the king being 'only the vicegerent or deputy of the Lord' — which is why no king of Judah made new laws or altered the old, a standing confession that God remained the supreme Legislator. And from the whole narrative he presses a political moral: 'an absolute monarchy is always an evil, and is contrary to all the rights, civil and religious, of mankind.' Characteristically, Clarke then sails past the text into a panegyric on limited monarchy as he knew it — king, nobles, and people 'duly mixed,' each restraining the other — an aside that tells us more about his own constitutional loyalties than about Ramah, but which honestly develops his central point: the tyranny Samuel described is the manner of unaccountable power, not of government as such.

Henry supplies the darker theological summary: 'Make them a king, and let them make their best of him' — 'so he gave them up to their own hearts' lusts.' Gill notes simply that the Lord spoke 'by an audible voice, or by an impulse upon his mind,' and Keil and Delitzsch that with this answer Samuel sent the men of Israel home until God should reveal the man. On the last word the witnesses converge: God's sovereignty is not defeated by his concessions. Israel gets its king; God keeps his throne; and out of a demand born of unbelief he will in time bring David, and David's greater Son — the King whose reign is the very theocracy Israel threw away, restored in mercy.
